commonProblems.h2: Common Rolls Royce Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Rolls Royce ownership operates in a specialist-led ecosystem where trust and precision engineering trump price sensitivity. The modern BMW-sourced N74 V12 (Phantom, Ghost) and the legendary L410 V8 (Silver Shadow, Silver Spirit) dominate replacement demand, while classic owners fear ‘death by a thousand cuts’ labour costs. Every rebuilt Rolls Royce engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Below, we break down the most common failures, real UK repair costs, and when replacement is the only sensible option.

problems.group: N74 V12 High-Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) Failure
problems.h4: N74 V12 High-Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) Failure – The Modern Rolls Royce Crisis
problems.rootCause: The BMW-sourced N74 V12 uses a ‘Hot-V’ design with twin turbos placed inside the engine valley. High-pressure fuel pumps (HPFP) suffer from internal wear and seal failure due to extreme heat cycling and poor fuel lubrication with modern UK low-sulphur diesel and ethanol-blended petrol. Failure causes sudden loss of power or complete no-start.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replacement of both high-pressure fuel pumps, fuel rail pressure sensors, and high-pressure fuel lines. Full system purge and leak test. | 30,000–50,000 miles. Suitable if caught immediately after first hesitation or long crank. Does not address underlying heat damage to injectors. |

| Supply & fit of a rebuilt N74 engine with upgraded HPFP units, revised injectors, and improved turbo oil feed lines. Bench tested with 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. | 80,000+ miles. The only sensible route if HPFP debris has contaminated injectors or if the engine has suffered a no-start condition. |

problems.recommendation: If your Phantom or Ghost experiences extended cranking or a drivetrain malfunction warning, replace the HPFPs immediately at a specialist — dealer pricing is excessive for this component-level job. Do not drive the car. Once the engine fails to start, the entire fuel system is typically contaminated, and a full rebuilt engine with new injectors and pumps is the only reliable long-term solution. On a car worth £40,000–£80,000, a £12k–£18k specialist replacement is vastly better than a £28k dealer bill.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt N74 V12 engine prices for your Phantom or Ghost

problems.group: N74 V12 Turbocharger Coking & Bearing Failure
problems.h4: N74 V12 Turbocharger Coking & Bearing Failure – The Hot-V Design Flaw
problems.rootCause: The ‘Hot-V’ layout places twin turbochargers directly inside the V of the engine. Oil drains through narrow passages that coke (carbonise) when the engine is switched off hot, blocking lubrication. Turbo bearings then fail catastrophically, often sending metal debris into the engine oil system and destroying the bottom end.

repairOptions.tier: | Preventative – Turbo Oil Line & Drain Cleaning |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Removal and chemical cleaning of turbo oil supply and drain lines. Replacement of oil cooler and full synthetic oil flush. Turbos inspected but not replaced. | Suitable only as preventative maintenance before any turbo noise or oil burning. Extends turbo life by 20,000–30,000 miles. |
| Replacement of both turbo cartridges (CHRAs), new oil lines, oil cooler, and three oil changes to flush debris. | 50,000–60,000 miles.
repairOptions.longevity: Viable if caught early with minor bearing noise and no engine knock. |

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of rebuilt N74 engine with upgraded turbo oil drain lines, larger capacity oil cooler, and revised turbo bearings. Fully bench tested.
repairOptions.longevity: 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. | The only reliable route if turbo bearing debris has reached the crankshaft journals or if the engine has developed a bottom-end knock. |

problems.recommendation: On any N74 V12 approaching 50,000 miles, have a specialist inspect the turbo oil drains during routine servicing. Preventative cleaning is cheap insurance. Once a turbo fails and sends debris through the oil system, do not attempt cartridge-only replacement — we consistently see secondary failure within 6 months. A full rebuilt engine with upgraded oil system components is the correct long-term answer for a Ghost or Wraith worth preserving.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt N74 V12 engines with upgraded turbo oil systems

problems.group: L410 V8 Crankshaft Thrust Washer Failure
problems.h4: L410 V8 Crankshaft Thrust Washer Failure – The Classic Silver Shadow & Silver Spirit Terminal Fault
problems.affectedModels: Rolls Royce Silver Shadow (1965–1980, L410 6.2L & 6.75L), Silver Spirit/Spur (1980–1998, L410 6.75L), Bentley Mulsanne/Turbo R (1980–1998, L410 6.75L)
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles (often after a recent clutch or transmission removal)
problems.rootCause: The L410 V8 uses bronze thrust washers on the crankshaft to control end-float. When the automatic transmission is removed or the torque converter is disturbed incorrectly, the washers can drop out of position. The crankshaft then moves forward, the washers disintegrate, and the crank grinds into the bearing cap. Oil pressure drops catastrophically, and the engine seizes.
problems.recommendation: If your Silver Shadow or Silver Spirit suddenly loses oil pressure after transmission work, stop the engine immediately. Do not restart. On a classic Rolls Royce worth £15,000–£35,000, a specialist rebuild at £12k–£18k is economically justifiable because no comparable car exists for the same money without significant hidden faults. The upgraded steel-backed thrust washers permanently solve the design flaw. Main dealers do not touch these engines — always use a specialist from our network.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt L410 V8 engine prices for your Silver Shadow or Silver Spirit

problems.group: L410 V8 Cylinder Liner Shift & Head Gasket Failure
problems.h4: L410 V8 Cylinder Liner Shift & Head Gasket Failure – The Wet Liner Weakness
problems.affectedModels: Rolls Royce Silver Shadow (1965–1980), Silver Spirit/Spur (1980–1998), Silver Seraph (1998–2002, BMW M73 V12 – different engine, included for balance), Corniche (1971–1987)
problems.rootCause: The L410 V8 uses removable wet cylinder liners. Over time or after a single overheating episode, the liner ‘O’ rings compress, allowing the liner to shift downward. Coolant then bypasses the top seal, filling the sump with coolant and causing pressurisation of the cooling system. White smoke, mayonnaise on the oil cap, and eventual hydrolock follow.
problems.recommendation: If you catch coolant in the oil early with no bearing knock, a full rebuild with top-hat liners is the correct answer. Head gaskets alone are a waste of money on an L410 — the liners will shift again within two years. On a cherished Silver Spirit, spend the £12k–£18k once and drive for decades. Use our marketplace to compare specialist rebuilders who specialise exclusively in L410 V8s.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt L410 V8 engines with top-hat liners

problems.group: N74 V12 Ignition Coil & Injector Failure
problems.h4: N74 V12 Ignition Coil & Injector Failure – The Misfire That Leads to Catalytic Converter Destruction
problems.affectedModels: Rolls Royce Phantom (2009–2017), Ghost (2010–2019), Wraith (2013–2019), Dawn (2015–2019)
problems.rootCause: The N74 V12 uses individual coil-on-plug ignition and BMW piezo injectors. Coils fail due to engine bay heat soak, and injectors stick open or closed. A single misfire, if ignored, dumps unburnt fuel into the exhaust, melting the expensive catalytic converters (£3k+ each from BMW). The engine management light will flash – not just illuminate – during a catalyst-damaging misfire.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Diagnosis, replacement of one failed ignition coil and one injector. Full adaptation reset. | 30,000–40,000 miles. Suitable only if caught immediately when the EML flashes. Do not drive more than 1 mile. |

repairOptions.tier: | Intermediate – Full Bank of Coils & Injectors |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replacement of all six coils and six injectors on one cylinder bank. Includes spark plugs, intake gaskets, and fuel rail resealing. | 60,000–80,000 miles. The sensible preventative approach when one coil/injector fails on a high-mileage N74. |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Repair – Complete Ignition & Fuel System |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replacement of all 12 coils, all 12 injectors, both HPFPs, all spark plugs, and both catalytic converters if damaged. | 80,000+ miles. The correct route if a misfire has been ignored and cats are now blocked. |

problems.recommendation: On any N74 V12, if the engine management light flashes, pull over immediately and call a specialist. A single mile of driving can turn a £400 coil repair into a £5,500 exhaust rebuild. When one coil fails at over 60,000 miles, we recommend replacing the entire bank of six coils and injectors — the labour is in the intake removal anyway. Use our comparison tool to find specialists who offer full N74 ignition system overhauls at half dealer prices.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for N74 V12 ignition system repair and rebuilt engines

problems.h4: OM706 / Deutz Diesel Engine Fuel Injection Pump Failure – Marine & Industrial Rolls Royce
problems.affectedModels: Rolls Royce OM706 marine engines, Rolls Royce Deutz BF6M units, Industrial gensets (1995–2010)
problems.rootCause: The OM706 and Deutz-sourced inline diesel engines use mechanically governed inline injection pumps. Over time, the pump camshaft and delivery valve seats wear, causing erratic fueling, hard starting, and white smoke. The engine will eventually run away on its own oil if the governor fails.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Injection Pump Calibration & Seal Kit |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Removal, strip, cleaning, seal replacement, recalibration on test bench, and refitting. | 2,000–3,000 hours.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable if the engine still starts and the governor is functional. |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt Pump or Engine |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply of a fully rebuilt injection pump (or full engine rebuild including pump, injectors, and turbo) with 12‑month warranty. | 5,000+ hours. The correct route if the governor has failed or the engine has suffered a runaway event. |

problems.recommendation: For marine or industrial Rolls Royce diesel owners, never attempt a DIY pump repair — incorrect calibration leads to engine overspeed and catastrophic failure. A proper test-bench recalibration by a specialist at £1,200–£2,000 is the minimum acceptable standard. If your engine has ever run away (uncontrolled high RPM), the entire engine must be rebuilt or replaced due to bottom-end damage. Our marketplace connects you with commercial diesel specialists across the UK.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt OM706 and Deutz marine engine prices

finalCta.h4: Don’t Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Rolls Royce
finalCta.paragraph: Whether you own a modern Phantom with an N74 V12 that has lost a turbo, or a classic Silver Shadow with an L410 that has dropped a thrust washer, the decision to repair or replace is never simple. EngineMarket makes it easy to compare fixed-price quotes from trusted UK Rolls Royce specialists who understand these engine families intimately. Every rebuilt engine in our network includes upgraded components designed to solve the original design flaw — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty and nationwide delivery. Compare prices today and get back on the road without the main dealer premium.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Rolls Royce Engine Replacement Prices Now
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates (£150–£250/hour) and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket’s UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Rolls Royce model and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty — always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineTypes.h2: Rolls Royce Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Rolls Royce engine type available through our UK network of trusted specialists, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your model, engine code and budget.

types.title: Rolls Royce Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A fully stripped, chemically cleaned and professionally inspected engine where all worn components (bearings, piston rings, gaskets, seals) are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Delivers factory reliability for N74 V12 and L410 V8 engines without main dealer pricing.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £2,500 – £9,000 (depending on engine code)
types.cta: Compare reconditioned Rolls Royce engine prices

types.title: Rolls Royce Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete teardown and rebuild using upgraded components specifically designed to eliminate known Rolls Royce failure points – including uprated high‑pressure fuel pumps (HPFP) for N74 V12s, reinforced timing chains for L410 V8s, and heavy‑duty turbo oil supply lines for hot‑V engines.
types.cta: Get rebuilt Rolls Royce engine quotes

types.title: Used Rolls Royce Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit removed from a donor vehicle with verifiable history (odometer reading, service records). The most budget‑friendly option for older models like Silver Shadow or Silver Spirit where the car's value doesn't justify a full rebuild.
types.cta: Check used Rolls Royce engine availability

types.title: Rolls Royce Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, zero‑mile engine: every surface is precision‑machined, every moving part is replaced (pistons, crankshaft, camshaft, oil pump), and the unit is assembled to meet exact OEM blueprints. The premium choice for Phantom, Ghost and Cullinan owners wanting as‑new perfection.
types.cta: Compare remanufactured Rolls Royce engine costs

types.title: Rolls Royce Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and compression‑tested engine that runs reliably but hasn't undergone a full rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast‑turnaround solution for budget‑conscious repairs on classic models (Corniche, Park Ward) where a full recon would exceed the vehicle's market value.
types.cta: View refurbished Rolls Royce engine options

types.title: Rolls Royce Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered directly to a UK Rolls Royce specialist, plus professional installation. Includes removal of the old unit, transfer of ancillaries (manifolds, turbochargers, sensors), new fluids, filters, gaskets, and a workmanship warranty.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £800 – £2,200 on top of engine price (varies by model and access complexity)
types.cta: Find Rolls Royce engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code (N74B66, N74B68, L410, N63), supplier location, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Rolls Royce model today.

engineSizes.h2: Rolls Royce Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Rolls Royce engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Rolls Royce 4.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The B70 4.2L V8 was Rolls Royce’s entry-level post-war petrol engine, appearing in the Silver Dawn and early Silver Cloud models. This inlet-over-exhaust (IOE) design – also known as the ‘F-head’ – is now extremely rare, and replacement demand is driven by worn camshafts, cracked cylinder heads, and obsolete parts availability. Most surviving B70 engines require full specialist rebuilds rather than simple replacement
items.cta: Compare Rolls Royce 4.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Rolls Royce 5.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The L410 5.3L V8 debuted in the 1965 Silver Shadow, replacing the ageing B70. This all-aluminium OHV engine introduced hydraulic tappets and a crossflow cylinder head design for smoother, quieter operation. Today, replacement demand is driven by cylinder liner shift, thrust washer failure, and general oil consumption. A properly rebuilt 5.3L L410 is exceptionally durable, but original high-mileage units are often terminal
items.cta: Compare Rolls Royce 5.3L petrol engine prices

items.title: Rolls Royce 6.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The L410 6.2L V8 was a displacement increase introduced to improve low-end torque for the heavier Silver Shadow and Bentley T-series. This is a ‘square’ engine (bore and stroke nearly equal) known for its exceptional smoothness but also for the same wet liner and thrust washer weaknesses as the 5.3L. Most L410 6.2L engines now require rebuilds, and original units with bottom-end knocking are only economically viable for full specialist overhaul
items.cta: Compare Rolls Royce 6.2L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: thrust washer failure on high-mileage cars

items.title: Rolls Royce 6.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The BMW-sourced N74B66 6.6L twin-turbo V12 powers the modern Ghost, Wraith, and Dawn. This ‘Hot-V’ engine places turbos inside the V of the engine for compact packaging, but this design creates extreme heat soak. Replacement demand is driven by high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure, turbo coking, and injector sticking. A rebuilt N74 from a specialist includes upgraded oil lines and revised fuel system components to permanently solve these weaknesses
items.cta: Compare Rolls Royce 6.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Rolls Royce 6.7L Petrol Engines (Classic L410)
items.description: The legendary L410 6.75L V8 is the longest-lived Rolls Royce engine, produced from 1970 to 1998 in Silver Shadows, Silver Spirits, and Bentleys. This is the ‘big block’ Rolls Royce engine – oversquare with huge torque. Replacement demand is driven by the same wet liner and crankshaft thrust washer failures as smaller L410 units, but parts availability is better. A factory-quality rebuild with top-hat liners and steel-backed thrust washers creates an engine that outlasts the car
items.cta: Compare Rolls Royce 6.7L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: thrust washer dropout on post-transmission work

items.title: Rolls Royce 6.7L Petrol Engines (Modern BMW N74)
items.description: The BMW N74B68 6.75L twin-turbo V12 is the final and largest modern Rolls Royce engine, fitted exclusively to the Phantom (2009–2022) and later Ghost (2020–present). This is an ‘ultimate refinement’ engine with closed-deck block design for stiffness. Replacement demand is driven by the same HPFP, turbo coking, and ignition issues as the 6.6L, plus additional complexity from cylinder deactivation (Active Cylinder Control). Specialist rebuilds with upgraded fuel and oil systems are strongly recommended over dealer exchange units
items.cta: Compare Rolls Royce 6.7L petrol engine prices

items.title: Rolls Royce Electric Motors
items.description: The Rolls Royce Spectre (2023–present) is the brand’s first series-production electric vehicle, using dual permanently excited synchronous motors (front and rear axle) producing 430kW (577bhp) and 900Nm of torque. Electric motor failure is extremely rare, but drive inverter electronics and thermal management system faults can occur. Replacement motors are specialist-order only through Rolls Royce or approved EV specialists. System / motor code(s): Permanently excited synchronous motors (proprietary Rolls Royce/BMW design, no public code)
items.cta: Enquire about Rolls Royce electric motor repair

engineSizes.closing: Not sure which engine size your Rolls Royce needs? Enter your registration number and we’ll match you to the exact engine code and displacement — instantly. All engines supplied with a minimum 12‑month warranty from UK Rolls Royce specialists.

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Rolls Royce Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Rolls Royce engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Rolls Royce specialists.

entries.title: N74B68 6.75L Twin-Turbo V12 Petrol
entries.description: Engine This 6,749cc direct-injection twin-turbo V12 powers the Phantom VIII (RR12/RR21, 2017–2022), Phantom VIII EWB, and Cullinan (2018–present). Output ranges from 563hp to 600hp. The most common catastrophic failures driving replacement are high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) seizure (sending metal debris through the fuel system) and turbocharger coking due to the hot-V design, which leads to oil starvation and bearing failure. A quality rebuilt unit includes upgraded HPFP components with revised metallurgy, new turbo oil supply and return lines with anti-coking coating, replacement injectors, and a full fuel system flush.
entries.cta: Get quotes for N74B68 Rolls Royce engine replacement

entries.title: N74B66 6.6L Twin-Turbo V12 Petrol
entries.description: Engine The 6,592cc N74B66 powers Ghost I (RR4, 2009–2020), Wraith (2013–2019), and Dawn (2015–2020). Output varies from 563hp to 624hp. The same HPFP seizure and turbo coking issues affect this engine as its larger N74B68 sibling, with premature HPFP wear accelerated by infrequent use (common on luxury GTs). Specialist rebuilds include upgraded HPFP internals, new injectors, revised turbo oil feed lines, and replacement of the oil pump pickup to remove carbon deposits.
entries.cta: Compare N74B66 Rolls Royce engine prices

entries.title: N74B60 6.0L Twin-Turbo V12 Petrol
entries.description: Engine Found in early Phantom VII (RR1, 2009–2012) before the displacement increase to 6.75L. This 5,972cc unit produces 453hp and 531lb-ft. HPFP failure and VANOS solenoid faults are the primary replacement drivers. Rebuilt units include upgraded HPFP components, new VANOS units, and revised oil check valves to prevent drain-back on startup.
entries.cta: Get quotes for N74B60 Rolls Royce engine replacement

families.name: Modern BMW-Sourced V8 Engines (Limited Rolls Royce Use, 2010–2016)

entries.title: N63B44 4.4L Twin-Turbo V8 Petrol
entries.description: Engine This 4,395cc twin-turbo V8 saw very limited Rolls Royce application, primarily in early Ghost I (RR4, 2010–2014) before the V12 became standard across the range. Output is 420–450hp. Common failures include hot-V oil leaks from valve cover gaskets and turbo oil lines, plus VANOS solenoid clogging from carbonised oil. Specialist rebuilt units include upgraded VANOS units, new valve cover gaskets, revised turbo oil feed lines with heat shielding, and a full oil system flush.
entries.cta: Compare N63B44 Rolls Royce engine prices

families.name: Classic L-Series V8 Engines (1959–2003, Still Commonly Replaced)

entries.title: L410I 6.75L Naturally Aspirated V8 Petrol
entries.description: Engine The legendary L410I 6,750cc pushrod V8 powered Silver Spirit (1980–1997), Silver Spur (1980–1997), Corniche (1981–1987), and Bentley Mulsanne (shared platform). Output ranges from 200hp to 300hp depending on carburetion and compression. Replacement drivers include cylinder head gasket failure (wet-liner seal degradation), timing chain stretch (single-row chain design), and age-related bore wear. A quality rebuilt unit includes upgraded composite head gaskets with ARP studs, a heavy-duty double-row timing chain conversion, hardened valve seats for unleaded fuel, and re-metalled bearings.
entries.cta: Get quotes for L410I Rolls Royce engine replacement

entries.title: L410 6.2L Naturally Aspirated V8 Petrol
entries.description: Engine The first-generation L410 6,230cc V8 appeared in Silver Shadow (1965–1977). Producing approximately 170-220hp depending on compression ratio (low-compression US export models vs high-compression UK cars). Common failures include timing chain stretch (requiring engine-out replacement) and overheating leading to cylinder head gasket failure. Rebuilt units include a new double-row timing chain and tensioner, resurfaced heads with upgraded gaskets, new valve stem seals, and a rebuilt SU carburettor set.
entries.cta: Compare L410 6.2L Rolls Royce engine prices

entries.title: B70 4.2L Naturally Aspirated V8 Petrol
entries.description: Engine The 4,257cc B70 (an early L-series variant) powered post-war Silver Dawn (1949–1955) and early Bentley MkVI. This inlet-over-exhaust pushrod V8 produces approximately 150hp. Failure drivers include bore wear, valve recession (original leaded fuel design running on modern unleaded), and cracked cylinder heads. Specialist rebuilds include hardened valve seats for unleaded fuel, new cylinder liners, and re-metalled white metal bearings.
entries.cta: Get quotes for B70 Rolls Royce engine replacement

entries.title: M73TUB54 5.4L Naturally Aspirated V12 Petrol
entries.description: Engine The 5,379cc M73TUB54 (shared with BMW 750iL) powered Silver Seraph (1998–2002) and Park Ward (2000–2002). Producing 322hp and 361lb-ft. Common failures driving replacement are VANOS solenoid failure, cooling system leaks (plastic water pump impeller failure), and oil separator clogging causing oil consumption. Rebuilt units include rebuilt VANOS units with revised seals, a new water pump with metal impeller, and an upgraded oil separator system.
entries.cta: Compare M73TUB54 Rolls Royce engine prices

entries.title: N73B60 6.0L Naturally Aspirated V12 Petrol
entries.description: Engine The 5,972cc N73B60 powered early Phantom VII (2003–2012, RR1). Produces 453hp and 531lb-ft. Replacement is driven by camshaft sensor failure (requiring timing cover removal), valvetronic motor failure, and oil consumption from worn valve stem seals. Quality rebuilt units include new camshaft position sensors, replacement valvetronic motors, upgraded valve stem seals (Viton material), and a complete timing chain set.
entries.cta: Get quotes for N73B60 Rolls Royce engine replacement

entries.title: 20/25 3.3L Naturally Aspirated Straight-6 Petrol
entries.description: Engine The 3,669cc 20/25 engine powered the Rolls Royce 20/25 (1929–1936). Produces approximately 25-30hp. Replacement (more accurately, full rebuild) is driven by age-related bore wear, dropped valve seats, and cracked cylinder heads. Specialist rebuilds are bespoke, costing significantly more than modern engines, and include new white metal bearings, cylinder sleeving, and valve seats compatible with modern unleaded fuel.
entries.cta: Enquire about 20/25 Rolls Royce engine rebuild (bespoke)

entries.title: Silver Ghost 7.4L Naturally Aspirated Straight-6 Petrol
entries.description: Engine The 7,428cc engine powered Silver Ghost (1906–1926). Produces 48-80hp depending on year. Replacement drivers include cracked blocks (age-related), worn crankshaft bearings, and valve gear wear. Rebuilds are extremely specialist, requiring pattern-making for discontinued parts and white metal bearing pouring. Our network includes pre-war specialists. Closing line (Block A): Can’t find your engine code listed above? Use our full Rolls Royce engine code directory below — every code from the last 120 years — or enter your registration number for an instant match to the correct replacement engine with 12-month unlimited mileage warranty.
entries.cta: Enquire about Silver Ghost Rolls Royce engine rebuild (bespoke)

engineCodeDirectory.closing: Can’t find your engine code listed above? Use our full Rolls Royce engine code directory below — every code from the last 120 years — or enter your registration number for an instant match to the correct replacement engine with 12-month unlimited mileage warranty.

directory.intro: A comprehensive alphabetical listing of every Rolls Royce engine code from the last 120 years. Use the search above or enter your registration for an instant match to your specific engine. All codes link to a quote request.

engineYears.h2: Rolls-Royce Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section covers every major Rolls-Royce engine change across all UK-market models from 1998 to the present, organised by year so owners can identify their exact engine family, understand when BMW's V12 architectures were introduced and evolved, and compare rebuilt or replacement engine prices from UK Rolls-Royce specialists.

years.preview: Silver Seraph: First V12 Since 1939 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The Silver Seraph (1998) marked a definitive break from the heritage L-Series V8 lineage, using a BMW-sourced 5.4-litre naturally aspirated V12 - the first twelve-cylinder Rolls-Royce since the 1939 Phantom III and the beginning of BMW's long engine partnership with the marque.
keyChanges: Silver Seraph launches with BMW M73B54 5.4-litre naturally aspirated V12 - entirely replacing the Rolls-Royce/Bentley L-Series V8 used in all previous models since the 1950s
Rolls-Royce's final Crewe-built production cars under Vickers/VW ownership; BMW acquires the Rolls-Royce name and marque rights in 1998 for £40m
Silver Spirit/Silver Dawn production ends; the Seraph represents the sole Rolls-Royce model in UK from 1998 to 2002
BMW 5-speed ZF automatic replaces previous 4-speed unit; adaptive ride control standard fitment
Park Ward long-wheelbase variant introduced alongside standard Seraph on same powertrain
mainEngines: BMW M73B54 - 5,379cc naturally aspirated V12 322–326bhp, 490Nm (Silver Seraph, Park Ward LWB)
knownFor: M73B54 is an exceptionally smooth and refined V12 - by any objective measure, the Seraph's powertrain is amongst the quietest in the world at this price point
Relatively modest 322bhp from a 5.4-litre unit reflects the era's low-state-of-tune philosophy - effortless, unhurried delivery rather than outright power
M73 maintenance costs are severe relative to power output - spark plug access across 12 cylinders is laborious; specialist scheduling recommended at every major service
Low volume production (2,138 total examples) means used-market specialist sourcing is the realistic route for any engine replacement - stock parts supply is limited
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Rolls-Royce Silver Seraph engine replacement UK - Rolls-Royce 5.4 V12 engine for sale - Rolls-Royce Silver Seraph engine rebuild specialist
years.cta: Compare 1998–2002 Rolls-Royce Silver Seraph V12 replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Phantom VII: BMW N73 V12, New Era Begins Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The Goodwood-built Phantom VII (2003) was the most important Rolls-Royce in decades, inaugurating a completely new BMW-developed N73 6.75-litre naturally aspirated V12 - a displacement that deliberately echoed the heritage Bentley 6.75-litre as a nod to the brand's English craftsmanship identity.
keyChanges: N73B68 6.75-litre naturally aspirated V12 introduced - 453bhp, 720Nm; DOHC 48-valve with Valvetronic variable valve lift and Double VANOS timing
Phantom VII built on entirely new aluminium space-frame architecture at the new Goodwood factory - no shared platform with any BMW production car
First Rolls-Royce from BMW's independent Goodwood operation; hand-built with bespoke engineering from the ground up
Phantom Drophead Coupé (2007) and Phantom Coupé (2008) extended the N73 into open and fixed-roof variants
ZF 6-speed automatic with steering-wheel paddles introduced; no manual option
mainEngines: BMW N73B68 - 6,749cc naturally aspirated V12 453bhp (Phantom VII Saloon, Drophead Coupé, Coupé)
knownFor: N73 is regarded by specialists as a magnificent but high-maintenance V12 - engine lifespan to 330,000km is achievable when oil intervals and VANOS service schedules are observed precisely
Double VANOS system is the N73's most significant mechanical weakness - actuator and solenoid failure begins from 90,000–150,000km; early symptoms include rough cold idle and timing rattle

Carbon deposits on intake valves are a known characteristic of the N73's direct injection system - intake manifold removal for valve cleaning every 40,000–50,000 miles is recommended by independent specialists

Blue smoke on cold start or after extended idling is a documented valve stem seal failure mode on higher-mileage N73 units - common from 80,000 miles onward, and generating the highest replacement enquiry volume for this generation
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Rolls-Royce Phantom V12 engine replacement UK - Rolls-Royce N73 engine for sale - Rolls-Royce Phantom engine rebuild specialist - Rolls-Royce 6.75 V12 VANOS failure repair - Rolls-Royce Phantom blue smoke valve seal repair
years.cta: Get quotes for 2003–2009 Rolls-Royce Phantom N73 V12 replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: Ghost Arrives: N74 Twin-Turbo V12 Launches Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The Ghost (2009) introduced an entirely new engine generation - the BMW N74 6.6-litre twin-turbocharged V12, producing 570bhp and fundamentally transforming Rolls-Royce's performance character while establishing the powerplant that would underpin the entire range for the next decade.
keyChanges: N74B66 6.6-litre twin-turbo V12 debuts in Ghost - 570bhp, 780Nm; DOHC 48-valve with twin turbochargers, the first forced-induction engine in a modern Rolls-Royce
Ghost becomes Rolls-Royce's most accessible and best-selling model in the brand's modern history, broadening the owner demographic significantly
N74 shares architecture with the N73 but adds twin turbocharging and revised direct injection management for increased output
Wraith (2013) adopts the same N74B66 in higher-output tune - 624bhp, the most powerful production Rolls-Royce to that date
Phantom VII continues with naturally aspirated N73B68 in parallel
ZF 8-speed automatic replaces 6-speed across Ghost and Wraith
mainEngines: BMW N73B68 - 6,749cc naturally aspirated V12 453bhp (Phantom VII, continued)
knownFor: N74 twin-turbo V12 is widely regarded as one of the finest production V12 engines ever built - 200,000–300,000 miles is achievable with correct maintenance, a landmark reliability figure for a forced-induction unit

High-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure is the N74's most common single-component fault - typically presents as extended cranking, rough running, or a no-start condition; known from 60,000–80,000 miles
Wastegate actuator rod wear causes a characteristic rattle from the turbocharger area - if unaddressed, this escalates to turbocharger failure requiring full unit replacement at significant cost
Carbon deposits on intake valves are shared with the N73 - direct injection without port injection means periodic walnut-blast cleaning is a necessary preventative procedure
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Rolls-Royce Ghost V12 engine replacement UK - Rolls-Royce N74 engine for sale - Rolls-Royce Wraith engine rebuild - Rolls-Royce Ghost turbo replacement - Rolls-Royce N74 fuel pump failure repair UK
years.cta: Compare 2009–2013 Rolls-Royce Ghost and Wraith N74 V12 replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Dawn, Phantom Extended & N73 Final Generation
years.description: This period saw Rolls-Royce extend both engine families into new bodystyles - the convertible Dawn (2015) received a revised N74, while the Phantom EWB and Phantom Series II continued the N73 naturally aspirated V12 in its most refined, final form.
keyChanges: Dawn convertible (2015) launched with N74B66 producing 570bhp - N74 now confirmed as the performance/entry engine across the range
Phantom Extended Wheelbase (EWB) and Phantom Series II continue with N73B68 naturally aspirated V12, unchanged in displacement and output
Ghost Series II (2014 facelift) adopts revised N74 with improved turbo cooling and fuel pressure management - addressing early HPFP concerns
Wraith Black Badge (2016) introduces the most powerful N74 derivative to date - 632bhp - first "performance" sub-brand for Rolls-Royce
Euro 6 emissions compliance achieved on N74 through revised injection and turbo mapping
mainEngines: BMW N74B66 - 6,592cc twin-turbo V12 570bhp (Ghost Series II, Dawn)
BMW N74B66 Black Badge - 6,592cc twin-turbo V12 632bhp (Wraith Black Badge)
BMW N73B68 - 6,749cc naturally aspirated V12 453bhp (Phantom EWB, Phantom Series II)
knownFor: Ghost Series II N74 refinements address the worst of the first-generation HPFP vulnerability - later 2014–2016 build cars have a measurably improved service history in UK specialist workshops
Dawn's open-body structure introduces additional NVH management requirements - engine mount and transmission tunnel sealing are more involved service tasks than on the closed Ghost
Wraith Black Badge N74 at 632bhp runs closer to thermal limits than standard Wraith - turbocharger maintenance and coolant circuit inspections are recommended at 60,000-mile intervals

N73 Phantom Series II is approaching the end of its production life - replacement part availability from OEM sources will narrow over the coming years, making specialist rebuilt engines increasingly valuable
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Rolls-Royce Dawn engine replacement UK - Rolls-Royce Ghost Series II N74 engine for sale - Rolls-Royce Wraith Black Badge engine rebuild - Rolls-Royce Phantom Series II engine specialist
years.cta: Find 2013–2016 Rolls-Royce Ghost, Dawn and Wraith replacement V12 engines from UK specialists

years.preview: Phantom VIII & Cullinan: N74 Becomes the Sole Engine Major Reliability Revision
years.description: The all-new Phantom VIII (2017) and Cullinan SUV (2018) retired the N73 naturally aspirated V12 for good, with the revised N74B66 twin-turbo now the single engine across every Rolls-Royce model - also featuring the most significant internal revisions to the N74 since its 2009 introduction.
keyChanges: Phantom VIII (2017) launches with revised N74B66 - 563bhp, 900Nm; new aluminium spaceframe (Architecture of Luxury) with extensive sound deadening
N73 naturally aspirated V12 officially discontinued - the Phantom VIII adoption of the twin-turbo N74 ends the last naturally aspirated Rolls-Royce V12
Cullinan SUV (2018) introduces N74B66 into the brand's first SUV body - 570bhp, air suspension standard; the N74's most thermally demanding application to that date
Revised N74 features improved HPFP specification, revised turbo oil feed lines, and updated crankcase ventilation - addressing accumulated knowledge from Ghost/Wraith service history
Ghost Black Badge, Wraith Black Badge, and Dawn Black Badge all continue with N74 in 632bhp tune
mainEngines: BMW N74B66 revised - 6,592cc twin-turbo V12 563–570bhp (Phantom VIII, Cullinan, Ghost, Dawn)
BMW N74B66 Black Badge - 6,592cc twin-turbo V12 632bhp (Ghost BB, Wraith BB, Dawn BB)
knownFor: Revised N74 in the Phantom VIII is the most refined and well-engineered application of the engine - low failure rates on early examples, with the Architecture of Luxury's thermal management significantly reducing under-bonnet heat soak

Cullinan's additional kerb weight (2,660kg) places the highest thermal loads on any road-going N74 - coolant system and turbocharger maintenance intervals should not be extended on high-mileage examples
With the N73 now discontinued, all Rolls-Royce engines from 2017 onward are N74 family - significantly simplifying the UK specialist parts and rebuild landscape
Phantom VIII N74 Oil leaks around valve cover gaskets are a documented early-ownership service item - often resolved under warranty but recurring on out-of-warranty cars
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Rolls-Royce Phantom VIII engine replacement UK - Rolls-Royce Cullinan V12 engine for sale - Rolls-Royce N74 engine rebuild specialist - Rolls-Royce Cullinan engine specialist UK - Rolls-Royce 2017 2018 2019 V12 engine
years.cta: Get quotes for 2017–2019 Rolls-Royce Phantom VIII and Cullinan N74 V12 replacement engines

years.preview: Ghost Series II All-New; Black Badge Expands Performance Landmark
years.description: The all-new Ghost (2020) introduced the most technically advanced N74 application to date with an additional front electric motor for torque vectoring, while the Black Badge performance sub-brand expanded to the Cullinan - Rolls-Royce's highest-volume, highest-specification N74 era.
keyChanges: All-new Ghost (2020) adopts N74B66 with Integrated Motor Generator (IMG) on the front axle - a first for Rolls-Royce; adds instant electric torque for improved low-speed refinement
Ghost now produces 571bhp with the hybrid motor assist; 0–62mph in 4.6 seconds - the most dynamic Ghost ever
Cullinan Black Badge (2020) brings N74 to 600bhp in the SUV body - the most powerful Cullinan to that date
Ghost Black Badge reaches 592bhp - a new landmark for the standard Ghost range
Phantom Extended (EWB) receives N74 revisions continuing from 2017 spec
Euro 6d final compliance across entire range
mainEngines: BMW N74B66 IMG - 6,592cc twin-turbo V12 + front electric motor 571bhp (Ghost Mk2)
BMW N74B66 Black Badge - 6,592cc twin-turbo V12 592–600bhp (Ghost BB Mk2, Cullinan BB)
BMW N74B66 - 6,592cc twin-turbo V12 563–570bhp (Phantom VIII, Cullinan standard, continued)
knownFor: All-new Ghost N74 IMG is the quietest and most technically accomplished iteration of the V12 - the added electric motor front axle torque vectoring virtually eliminates any driveline shudder or hesitation at low speed
Integrated Motor Generator adds a new complexity layer not present on pre-2020 Rolls-Royces - diagnostic equipment must be capable of the hybrid front-axle system as well as the combustion V12
High-pressure fuel pump and wastegate rattle remain the N74's documented service considerations in this period, though improved manufacturing tolerances reduce incidence on 2020+ build cars
Ghost Mk2 and Cullinan Black Badge now generate the highest replacement enquiry volumes in UK specialist workshops - driven by growing used-market volumes as first owners begin second-cycle sales
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Rolls-Royce Ghost 2020 engine replacement UK - Rolls-Royce Ghost Black Badge engine for sale - Rolls-Royce Cullinan Black Badge engine rebuild - Rolls-Royce N74 specialist UK - Rolls-Royce 6.6 V12 twin turbo engine
years.cta: Compare 2020–2022 Rolls-Royce Ghost and Cullinan N74 V12 replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Spectre Electric: The First Battery-Powered Rolls-Royce Technology Introduction
years.description: The Spectre (2023 UK on sale) became the first all-electric Rolls-Royce and the first BEV ultra-luxury coupé in the world - using twin synchronous electric motors and a 102kWh battery to produce 577bhp, while all combustion models continue with the established N74 V12 awaiting the brand's full electrification transition by 2030.
keyChanges: Rolls-Royce Spectre BEV launches UK 2023 - twin motor AWD, 577bhp, 664Nm, 0–62mph in 4.4 seconds, 260-mile WLTP range
102kWh liquid-cooled lithium-ion battery; 400V architecture; 195kW peak DC fast-charge
Spectre built on same Architecture of Luxury spaceframe as Phantom and Cullinan - engineered from inception with EV capability
Ghost, Cullinan, and Phantom continue with N74B66 and Black Badge variants unchanged alongside the Spectre
Rolls-Royce commits to 100% electric range by 2030 - confirmed at brand level; all future nameplates to be BEV only
Main Engines / Powertrains
Dual synchronous AC motors - front 190kW (255hp), rear 360kW (483hp), combined 577bhp / 664Nm (Rolls-Royce Spectre BEV)
BMW N74B66 - 6,592cc twin-turbo V12 563–571bhp (Cullinan, Ghost Mk2, Phantom VIII continuation)
BMW N74B66 Black Badge - 6,592cc 592–600bhp (Ghost BB, Cullinan BB)
knownFor: Spectre's 577bhp electric architecture makes it the fastest production Rolls-Royce in a straight line - yet the silent powertrain delivers what Rolls-Royce describe as "effortless waftability" with zero combustion noise
Battery and electric motor replacement for the Spectre is early-stage for UK independent specialists - Rolls-Royce main dealer network currently holds the primary service advantage on BEV diagnostics

N74 combustion V12 replacement enquiries remain the dominant workflow for UK Rolls-Royce specialists through this period as the large population of 2009–2020 Ghost, Wraith, and Cullinan cars reaches high mileage

The 2030 full-electric transition commitment means the N74 is now definitively in end-of-production life - securing a quality rebuilt N74 unit for a used Rolls-Royce is a long-term value-preservation decision
years.cta: Enquire about 2023-onwards Rolls-Royce Spectre BEV drivetrain and N74 V12 replacement options from UK specialists

engineYears.closing: Not sure which year your Rolls-Royce was built? Enter your registration number and we'll identify the exact engine, model year, and compatible replacement options instantly - all backed by a minimum 12-month warranty from UK Rolls-Royce specialists.

faq.h2: Rolls Royce Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Rolls Royce owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Rolls Royce engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A rebuilt Rolls Royce engine typically costs between £12,000 and £22,000 supply‑only, depending on the engine family. The legendary L410 V8 (Silver Shadow, Silver Spirit) commonly falls in the £12,000–£18,000 range, while the modern BMW‑sourced N74 twin‑turbo V12 (Phantom, Ghost) sits higher at £15,000–£22,000. Supply and fit adds approximately £1,500–£3,000 depending on the model and workshop location. Used engines are cheaper at £5,000–£10,000 but carry no warranty and unknown history — we strongly recommend a rebuilt unit with upgraded components for any Rolls Royce you intend to keep.
items.cta: Compare Rolls Royce engine replacement prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Rolls Royce engine replacement?
items.answer: Four key factors determine your final bill: engine size and complexity (a 6.75L N74 V12 costs significantly more than a 6.2L L410 V8), whether you choose used, reconditioned, or rebuilt, vehicle age and complexity (modern Ghost engines require more labour hours than classic Silver Shadows), and whether you need supply‑only or supply‑and‑fit. Location also matters — London and South‑East specialists typically charge 20–30% more than those in the Midlands or North. Our comparison tool provides transparent, fixed‑price quotes from multiple UK specialists so you can see exactly what affects your specific quote.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Rolls Royce engine specialists

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Rolls Royce?
items.answer: In most cases, yes — especially if the chassis, gearbox, and interior are in excellent condition. A properly rebuilt Rolls Royce engine with upgraded components (top‑hat liners on L410, revised turbo oil lines on N74) often outlasts the original. For a Silver Shadow worth £15,000–£25,000, a £12,000–£18,000 specialist rebuild is economically justifiable because no comparable car exists for the same money without hidden faults. For a modern Ghost worth £50,000–£80,000, replacement is almost always the correct decision. Only consider scrapping if the car has extensive structural rot or accident damage.
items.cta: Compare rebuild vs replacement costs for your Rolls Royce model

items.question: What's included in a Rolls Royce engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply‑and‑fit service includes sourcing the rebuilt or reconditioned engine, removing your old unit, installing the replacement with all new gaskets, seals, fluids, and filters, a workmanship warranty (typically 12 months), disposal of the old engine, and a road test to confirm performance. Fitting costs for Rolls Royce engines typically range from £1,500–£3,000. Always confirm exactly what is included — some specialists exclude ancillaries like alternators, power steering pumps, or engine mounts. Our network suppliers provide itemised quotes so there are no surprises.
items.cta: Compare Rolls Royce engine supply and fit deals

items.question: How long does a Rolls Royce engine replacement take?
items.answer: For most Rolls Royce models, a straightforward like‑for‑like engine swap takes 3–5 working days at a specialist workshop. More complex models — particularly modern N74 V12s with extensive electronics — can take 5–7 working days. The biggest variable is engine availability: common codes like the L410 6.75L or N74B66 are often in stock at major UK specialists, while rarer variants may require 2–4 weeks for a full rebuild. Always ask your supplier for a confirmed lead time before committing. Our network shows real‑time availability and estimated turnaround for each quoted engine.
items.cta: Find rebuilt Rolls Royce engines with fast UK delivery

items.question: Do reconditioned Rolls Royce engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes — every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty as standard. Many specialists also offer extended 24‑month coverage on fully rebuilt and remanufactured units (those with new pistons, bearings, timing chains, and upgraded components). The warranty typically covers major internal components: crankshaft, rods, pistons, camshafts, timing chain, oil pump, and cylinder head. Wear items like spark plugs, belts, and external seals are usually excluded. Always confirm exact warranty terms directly with your chosen supplier.
items.cta: Get quotes for Rolls Royce rebuilt engines with comprehensive warranty cover

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Rolls Royce engine?
items.answer: Used engines are cheaper upfront (£5,000–£10,000) and make sense if you need a temporary fix, plan to sell the car within a year, or have a very tight budget. However, they come with no warranty and unknown history — including the same original design flaws that killed your first engine. Reconditioned or rebuilt engines (£12,000–£22,000) cost more but include new piston rings, bearings, seals, gaskets, and upgraded components specifically designed to address known weaknesses. For any Rolls Royce you intend to keep long‑term, a rebuilt engine with a warranty is nearly always the better value.
items.cta: Use our comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Rolls Royce engine makes financial sense

items.question: Where can I find Rolls Royce engine specialists near me?
items.answer: EngineMarket connects you with a UK‑wide network of Rolls Royce‑approved and independent specialists who work exclusively on these precision engines. While you’ll find higher concentrations of L410 specialists in the Midlands and N74 specialists around London and the South‑East, our platform covers every region including Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Simply enter your registration number or select your model and engine code; we’ll match you with nearby suppliers who stock your specific engine and offer fitting. Nationwide delivery is also available if you prefer supply‑only and have your own trusted garage.
items.cta: Find Rolls Royce engine specialists near you

items.question: What are the most common Rolls Royce engine failures?
items.answer: The most common catastrophic failures driving Rolls Royce engine replacement are: N74 V12 high‑pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure (Phantom, Ghost, Wraith, Dawn — sudden no‑start), N74 V12 turbo coking due to ‘Hot‑V’ oil drain blockage (same models, progressive power loss then total failure), L410 V8 wet cylinder liner shift (Silver Shadow, Silver Spirit — coolant mixing with oil), and L410 V8 crankshaft thrust washer dropout (often after transmission work — sudden oil pressure loss and seizure). All four typically lead to full replacement rather than repair.
items.cta: Compare rebuilt engines for your specific Rolls Royce failure type

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Rolls Royce engine?
items.answer: Compare at least three quotes — prices for the same N74 or L410 engine can vary by £3,000–£5,000 between suppliers. Consider a used engine (£5,000–£10,000) if you have a tight budget and plan to sell the car soon. For long‑term ownership, invest in a rebuilt unit (£12,000–£22,000) with upgraded components — it will outlast the original. Ask about supply‑only if you have a trusted independent garage that can handle the swap for less than specialist fitting rates. Always check whether delivery, fitting, and VAT are included in the quoted price.
